text: Hemingway ended each writing session mid-sentence — deliberately, never at a natural stopping point. The technique was practical psychology: a sentence already begun is far easier to resume than a blank page. He worked from dawn until around noon at a standing desk in Havana, tracking his daily word count on a chart tacked to the wall. This external record served as both accountability and momentum. The ritual was non-negotiable: same hour, same posture, same physical record-keeping. What looked like habit was actually a precision instrument for bypassing the resistance that accumulates when creative work is treated as optional.
question: What was Hemingway's reason for stopping a writing session mid-sentence?

options.text: To signal his editor that a chapter was unfinished and needed review
To preserve narrative tension so readers would keep turning pages
To make it easier to resume the next session without facing a blank page
To force himself to reconsider word choices before committing to them
